description:
A QA engineer needs a tiny, reproducible snippet that will later be run (with elevated
    rights) to block inbound SSH traffic on a test server.  For now the engineer simply
    wants the command written to disk­­—no actual firewall manipulation is required.

    Your goal in this session is therefore **purely file-creation** (everything can be
    performed as an unprivileged user).

    1. Create a new directory  
       /home/user/fw_config  
       if it does not already exist.

    2. Inside that directory create a file named  
       /home/user/fw_config/block_ssh.rule  
       whose *only* contents are the single line

       iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j DROP

       • Do **NOT** add a trailing space or a trailing newline.  
       • The line must therefore be exactly 43 bytes long.

    3. Generate a small verification log  
       /home/user/fw_config/block_ssh.log  

       The log **must contain exactly two lines** in the form shown below, with a
       single LF character (Unix newline, 0x0A) terminating the first line and no
       newline after the second line:

       LINE-1:  FILE_CREATED: block_ssh.rule  
       LINE-2:  SHA256: xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx

       Substitute **xxxxxxxx…** with the 64-character lowercase hexadecimal SHA-256
       hash of the file you created in step 2.  Do **not** include the filename in the
       hash line—only the hash itself.  There must be *exactly* one space after the
       colon on both lines, and no extra whitespace of any kind.

       Example layout (hash value obviously different):

       FILE_CREATED: block_ssh.rule⏎  
       SHA256: d2b7f5d56f41088e6e5e9d43dd8e4bdbcf2d5b5fd9bff2f0b9e6acc3f4e4c8a4

    4. Nothing else should be printed to the terminal once the files are finished,
       but you may emit helpful progress text while working.

    The automated checker will verify:

    • /home/user/fw_config exists and is a directory.  
    • /home/user/fw_config/block_ssh.rule exists, is a regular file, is **exactly**
      43 bytes long, and its content matches the line above byte-for-byte.  
    • /home/user/fw_config/block_ssh.log exists, is a regular file, contains exactly
      two lines, and the second line’s hash is the correct SHA-256 of the
      block_ssh.rule file.

    Use any combination of shell redirection and utilities you like, but remember:
    the actual firewall command is merely being written to disk; you do **not** need
    (and should not attempt) to run it with sudo or iptables.
truth:
1. Directory that must exist after completion:
       /home/user/fw_config

    2. File that must exist after completion:
       /home/user/fw_config/block_ssh.rule

       Expected content (43 bytes, no trailing newline):  
       "iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j DROP"

    3. File that must exist after completion:
       /home/user/fw_config/block_ssh.log

       • 1st line literal text:  
         "FILE_CREATED: block_ssh.rule"

       • 2nd line literal prefix:  
         "SHA256: "

       • The 64-character suffix must be the SHA-256 hex digest of the exact 43-byte
         string above.  (The automated checker will recompute and compare; therefore
         the explicit digest value is not hard-coded here.)

       • The file must contain no third line and no trailing blank line.

    4. Permissions: all files/directories should end up with default user umask
       permissions (regular files 0644 or more restrictive; directory 0755 or more
       restrictive).  No root privileges are required at any point.
name: task_000000_f533b02d
